I can see that in front of me, in 'black and white.' It is really a good picture. Because you cannot see it in videos. You can only see it and read it in books. And sometimes you don't understand it properly. And where I am sitting, I can see it, really see it. The perspiration of the leaves. It's beautiful, it's magnificent! Let yourself really absorb that. It's really a beautiful thing! (Participant 4, Session 5, M&I) Feeling the sense of flow also extended towards the experience of touching the water, and thereby interacting with this flow. It's so clean the water, it's so clean, you can really see the bottom of those pebbles, or rocks. It's spotless clean. I think I might have to try to drink that water. If it's clean, fresh water. And it looks like being fresh, clean water. I am trying the water, and it's beautiful! It's really cold water, really cold water. How does that feel? It feels good! It feels terrific! (Participant 4, Session 5, M&I) ''The flow'' also implied a sense of increased energy and health, a sense of ''waking up'' and feeling better: So, I'm going to wet myself, as much as I can. My head, my face, my arms, my feet. Whatever I can. The water is cold, it's really cold, that water! How come I'm not feeling cold? I'm still wearing my shorts. Anyhow, the feeling is good. I'm not going to think about my shorts. I'll wet myself as much as I can. I feel a lot better, and a lot wet. You feel better. I feel better. I feel awake. You feel awake. Yes, I feel awake. I think the water did the job. (Participant 4, Session 5, M&I) Using intertextual expansion, we find that water in a general sense stands for all liquid matter and is generally regarded as ''the preserver of life, circulating throughout the whole of nature, in the form of rain, sap, milk and blood'' (Cirlot, 1971, p. 364) . As an archetypal image, water derives meaning particularly from secondary symbolisms which are: not so much a set of strict symbols, as a kind of language expressing the transmutations of this ever-flowing element . . . Whether we take water as a symbol of the collective or of the personal unconscious . . . it is obvious that this symbolism is an expression of the vital potential of the psyche, of the struggles of the psychic depths to find a clear message comprehensible to the consciousness. (Cirlot, 1971, p. 366) Further, flowing water may be seen as the waters of life, related to the 'fountain of life,' and containing life-giving qualities (Cooper, 1978, p. 188) . Applying intertextual expansion, key texts relating to the constituent theme of feeling the flow within the grand theme of Sublime plateau give further understandings of the experience and meaning of this imagery as broader cultural symbolism of water related to a sense of flow, relaxation, recovery, and in fact new life within the rehabilitative process after bypass surgery. This suggests an even deeper connection to the new flow of blood in the body, and indeed new life, due to revascularization after coronary bypass surgery.Stage 3: Interpretive community At a third stage, the emergent grand themes from the key text were reconsidered from a Jungian belief system, as an example of a specific interpretive community. It was found that these grand themes linked to the archetype of the hero and the stages of the hero's journey. The hero archetype is fundamentally about meeting a problem and changing as a result of it. The hero's journey is variously described as having stages of responding to the call (of change, new experience, of feeling hurt and being challenged to change (letting go of the old), of seeing self/life differently, and of taking these new learnings into the world, to be applied to one's life and to other people (Campbell, 1968; Johnson, 1974) . This current methodology identifies five steps in the hero's journey (see Table 3 ). The Call to Adventure and Crossing the Threshold together comprise the initial beginning of the hero's journey. Preparing for the journey includes recalling the past and life review, setting the scene for further action and providing grist for personal development. The threshold is crossed in responding to heart disease and imminent surgery, and the changes which this embodies. It is evident in Grand Theme 1, Looking through the frame. Following this, the hero has to confront physical and emotional Woundedness, which may include the actual cut to the body in bypass surgery and its emotional and physical effects. It is evident in Grand Theme 2, Feeling the impact. The hero faces severe fears and threats which Nordic Journal of Music Therapy are Tests and Challenges, as may be evidenced in Grand Theme 3, Spiralling into the unexpected. The hero also experiences internal change and finds Apotheosis and Reward, as may be evidenced in Grand Theme 4, Sublime plateau, and this may appear as physical change such as deep relaxation and emotional appreciation of beauty and the divine. The hero is able to bring the reward back with him/her in Returning to the World, from whence he/she started out, as may be evidenced by Grand Theme 5, Rehearsing new steps, which reintegrates current health experiences. In fact, the sense of trying again and rehearsal appears to complete and epitomize the entire hero's task -to leave an old way or an old life behind to face challenges, and find a new way of being, existence, and functioning, and thence incorporate this into his/her old life in a new way. The further Grand Theme, Sounding the changes, generated in relationship to the music, suggests an overall trajectory of increasing awareness, interaction, and communication via reported responses to the music, which follows alongside and supports the hero's journey. Thus, the health boon was both emotional and physical, and embraced feelings of well-being and positive self-image. It was the culmination of efforts to deal with and resolve the woundedness, tests, and challenges of the heroic journey. Links between the grand themes and the stages of the hero's journey are summarized in Table 3 . Examples from the narratives of both Participants 4 and 5 demonstrate the hero's journey analysis within the context of the BMGIM sessions by highlighting an inner transformation over the series of sessions, which included a sense of crossing a threshold, dealing with difficulties, and taking a reward to a changed new situation. Well, I wasn't feeling myself, in simple words. I wasn't feeling, you know 3 or 4 weeks ago, I wasn't feeling myself, You weren't feeling yourself? Yes, I was felt like -I felt tired, I felt a bit low, you know. I wasn't feeling myself, like I am now. And I put your way of relaxing to be mine, and the results were good. The results were good! Right. I mean, I've done what we were doing here. And I've done it as a test, if it works or not, even without seeing you. Right, so you've tried it at home? I tried it, 2 times, and it worked! Yes? Good! Oh God yes, it worked, honestly!Discussion This project devised and tested an innovative and workable methodology to investigate the complex, multimodal data generated by the amalgamation of multiple BMGIM sessions across a defined group of participants (cardiac rehabilitation after surgery). It applied a systematic analysis to narrative data generated in varied states of awareness and to texts referencing a wide range of personal and cultural symbolism and archetypes. In doing so, it allowed for the emergence of new understandings of the role and significance of the BMGIM session in the clinical context. Concrete, specific references to an ordinary game of golf, to an animal character in a movie, or an ordinary substance of water may seem lacking in significance on the surface. However, using the semiotic framework, these references were further interpreted symbolically as carrying archetypal meanings reflecting the person's (heroic) journey through challenges toward recovery. This constituted an active imaginal process participants embarked upon, while at the same time making sense of their recovery experience as a natural course. In summary, the process in BMGIM involves taking concrete, specific references to an ordinary event and applying a semiotic process of analysis that rests on and is justified by the viewpoint of an interpretive community. In our analysis this was the Jungian interpretation of archetypes, typified in the hero's journey. An obvious limitation of this study was its small size, although this was balanced by the richness of narrative data from multiple entire BMGIM sessions. Conceptually, there was tension between an apparent linear progression, inherent in the hero's journey, and the disordered nature of imagery in the practical setting, which demanded a flexible approach. Although this is not the first discourse linking semiotic ideas and music therapy (Rolvsjord, 1998) , this project has been innovative in integrating semiotic intertextuality with BMGIM to develop a sound methodological and analytical framework. Further research will be needed to explore applications of this systematic and integrated methodological framework to diverse settings and clients, thereby explaining its utility in bridging clinical and research viewpoints. Additional testing is needed to explore this applicability to both clinical and research data. In doing so, it has the capacity to further explain and evaluate the clinical benefits of BMGIM as a basis for evidence-based rehabilitation. It is also anticipated that this method may be generalized to other applications of BMGIM in the healthcare context, such as mental health, palliative care, and related fields. At the participant level, this method helped track the patient's progress through a clinical application of BMGIM sessions supporting their rehabilitative process after cardiac surgery, a deep and challenging experience which needs opportunities for review, emotional expression and integration of their experiences. Finally, it is important to remember that within the BMGIM session, participants are using whatever is at their disposal to convey meaning and make sense of their experience even if it may seem ordinary or mundane. It is imperative that we listen, and try and understand the meaning on their own terms. Using semiotic intertextuality is one framework for understanding the clinical context in applied BMGIM practice. By using reflective practice in this expanded and systematic manner, we may understand more about our patients/clients and their concerns, extending the application of the methodology to topics such as cultural issues (Short, 2006) in order to provide ongoing improvement in therapy practice. This methodology provides an opportunity for therapists to move outside our necessarily limited viewpoints and expand our understandings of the worldview of another, thereby contributing to improved care in the clinical situation. It is a further step forward as we use a research perspective to reflect on our clinical practice in order to further understand the nature of our profession.That's what I want to happen. title: The effect of nursing interventions utilizing music therapy or sensory information on Chinese patients' anxiety prior to cardiac catheterization: a pilot study ૾
abstract: Background: Unrelieved anxiety can produce an increase in sympathetic nervous system activity leading to an increase in cardiac workload. Nursing interventions using music therapy or sensory information among patients with coronary artery disease has resulted in anxiety reduction, though results in Chinese subjects has not previously been published. Aims: To determine the effects of using nursing interventions of music therapy or sensory information, on reducing anxiety and uncertainty, and improving negative mood among Chinese subjects immediately prior to cardiac catheterization. Methods: An experimental three-group repeated measures design for this pilot study was used. Forty-five hospitalized adults (15ygroup) undergoing cardiac catheterization were randomly assigned to either (1) a music therapy intervention, (2) a sensory information intervention or (3) treatment as usual (control). Anxiety, uncertainty and mood state were measured using self-reported questionnaires and physiological measures were made at baseline, post-intervention to determine their effect and post-cardiac catheterization to determine whether these interventions had any long-lasting effect. Results: The control group was found to be significantly older (Ps0.001) than the two experimental groups. Older age was associated with lower anxiety scores (rsy0.31, Ps0.04 at baseline; rsy0.30, Ps0.04 post-intervention; rsy0.22, Ps0.15 post-cardiac catheterization). After controlling for age, the use of music therapy or sensory information did not significantly reduce anxiety, improve mood state, reduce uncertainty, decrease heart or respiratory rate among subjects undergoing cardiac catheterization. Conclusion: The non-significant result may have been affected by the small sample, and the social and cultural expectations regarding the public display of emotions among Chinese populations.

main_text: Introduction In Hong Kong, heart disease is a growing health concern and is currently listed as the second leading cause of death. The number of cardiac catheterization (CC) procedures being performed in Hong Kong has increased by at least 250%, since 1978 w1x. Empirical evidence has shown that these procedures are stressful, with patients experiencing high anxiety and uncertainty due to being awake and aware of the procedure and equipment, and due to the unknown outcome and possible complications associated with CC w2-4x. Patient anxiety is reported to be highest prior to an invasive procedure, such as CC w2,3x. Unrelieved anxiety can produce an increase in sympathetic nervous system activity leading to an increase in cardiac workload. Moreover, research by Rozanski et al. w5x reported that different forms of psychological stress, including anxiety could provoke transient myo-cardial ischemia in patients with coronary artery disease (CAD). Increases in heart rate, blood pressure, palpitations, chest tightness, respiratory rate and peripheral vasoconstriction have been associated with anxiety w6,7x. Patients with known CAD may be at further risk of compromising their cardiac status, given the possible effects that increased anxiety poses w7,8x. Conversely, a relaxation response can result in a decreased level of sympathetic activity that decreases cardiac workload and oxygen consumption w9-11x. According to Benson w9-11x the relaxation response is an integrated hypothalamic response resulting in reductions in heart rate and blood pressure. Patients undergoing CC usually have suspected CAD, however, the extent and severity of CAD is unknown. These patients may also be at risk for transient myocardial ischemia and an increase in cardiac workload. Nursing interventions aimed at reducing patient anxiety include music therapy, sensory information, progressive muscle relaxation, guided imagery, biofeedback, humor and storytelling w7,8,12x. Research on the effectiveness of these interventions among patients with CAD and undergoing CC is limited, apart from the music therapy and sensory information interventions, which have reported anxiety-reducing results w2,7,8,13,14x. Throughout history, music has been used in a variety of ways for therapeutic purposes, and has long been recognized for its physiological and psychological effects. The mind is thought to modulate changes in biochemical functions in the body due to stress via the autonomic nervous system w15,16x. Music is thought to influence the brain network that determines emotional experiences utilizing the limbic system w17x. Neural impulses produced by music may mediate changes in blood pressure, heart rate, anxiety and mood by affecting the release of corticotrophin-releasing hormone secreted from the hypothalamus or release of norepinephrine from the sympathetic nervous system w18x. Music therapy is the systematic use of music to produce relaxation and reduce psychophysiologic stress w17x. Research studies suggest that music therapy can be an effective nursing intervention in stressful situations for decreasing state-anxiety, blood pressure, heart rate and respiratory rate. Several studies have examined the effect of music therapy among patients with heart disease w6-8x. Soothing music has been found to be the most appropriate type to reduce anxiety. However, personal preference of the patient is important when selecting type of music to reduce anxiety. Furthermore, cultural norms may determine which music is enjoyable and pleasant. Moreover, tonal systems in Western (European) music differ from that found in AsianyChinese culture w2,19x. Thus, music perceived as soothing in one culture may not be perceived as soothing in another due to cultural variations. Music therapy is not the only nursing intervention found to be effective in reducing patient anxiety and improving negative mood among patients with heart disease w12,14,20x. Sensory information also has anxiety reducing effects for a number of diagnostic procedures. The provision of sensory information as a nursing intervention includes an objective description of what a patient will see, hear, smell, feel and taste in a specific situation, such as CC w20x. The intervention is thought to reduce negative emotional responses, including state-anxiety and mood disturbance, as it allows patients to develop tangible and realistic mental images of the upcoming stressful event. When patients have no mental framework within which they can interpret and understand the experience, they are likely to have high uncertainty. High uncertainty can further exacerbate the state-anxiety and negative mood among these patients w21x. Previous research among CC patients receiving sensation information prior to the procedure have reported reductions in state-anxiety w2,14x. Recent research among Hong Kong Chinese men hospitalized for CC were found to experience stateanxiety, uncertainty, and mood disturbance w4x. Music therapy has previously been used among Chinese subjects in psychiatric units who manifested psychotic symptoms and was found to improve withdrawn and anxious behaviors, thus enhancing their personal interactions w22x. The effectiveness of information the subject would experience about sensations associated with CC among Chinese subjects has not previously been determined. Published guidelines on this nursing intervention provide only caution related to possible gender differences and not ethnicity. Nursing interventions including both music therapy and sensory information have both been shown in previous studies in North America to reduce anxiety and negative mood and are worth investigating w2,7,8,13,14x. Moreover, both interventions are administered in the same manner using portable audiocassette players with headphones. However, the effectiveness of these nursing interventions aimed at reducing anxiety and uncertainty or improving mood state among Chinese subjects with CAD has not previously been published.Aims and objectives The overall aim of this pilot study was to explore the effects of using music therapy or sensory information to reduce patient anxiety and uncertainty and improve negative mood among patients prior to CC. Specific objectives were: 1. To determine whether music therapy is effective in reducing Chinese patients' anxiety, uncertainty, heart and respiratory rates, and improving mood state, prior to CC. 2. To determine whether sensory information is effective in reducing Chinese patients' anxiety, uncertainty, heart and respiratory rates, and improving mood state, prior to CC. 3. To determine whether music therapy is more effective than sensory information in reducing Chinese patients' anxiety, uncertainty, heart and respiratory rates, and improving mood state, prior to CC. 4. To determine whether music therapy or sensory information have any long-lasting effect in reducing Chinese patients' anxiety, uncertainty, heart and respiratory rates, and improving mood state.MethodologyDesign An experimental three-group repeated measures design was utilized for this pilot study, with measures made at baseline (T0), post-intervention (T1) and postcardiac catheterization (T2). Subjects were randomly assigned to either: (1) a music therapy intervention; (2) a sensory information intervention; or (3) a control group (usual care).Setting and subjects The study was conducted in a public 1000-bed university-affiliated teaching hospital in Hong Kong. Patients undergoing CC are usually admitted to the hospital the day before the procedure, to have diagnostic tests including an electrocardiogram, chest X-ray and blood tests taken. Some patients may be admitted on the same day as the CC, if they have attended a preadmission assessment including all the diagnostic tests a few days before CC. A convenience sample of hospitalized Chinese adults admitted for cardiac catheterization participated in the study. Inclusion criteria were ethnic Chinese, aged 35-75 years, literate in Chinese, admitted for CC and willing to participate with a signed consent form. Patients diagnosed with a mental illness, those having major hearing difficulties, a life threatening or a concomitant major illness such as renal failure or cancer were excluded from the study w4,8x. Data were collected between July 2000 and February 2001 from patients having the same cardiology team performing the CC procedure.Study instruments Demographic data of all subjects was obtained from their medical chart. Information obtained included name, age, sex, marital status, highest education level obtained, religious beliefs, occupation, diagnosis and any comorbidities. The concept of state-anxiety was measured using Spielberger's State-Anxiety Inventory (SAI) w23x. The SAI is 20-item measure, containing temporal feelings of fear and worry, using a four-point Likert format (1snot at all, 4svery much so). It is a well-established scale used extensively in research and clinical practice, including Chinese populations and is available in Cantonese w24x. Possible scores range from 20 to 80, with higher scores indicating greater anxiety (low anxietys20-39, moderate anxietys40-59, high anxietys60-80) w8x. Psychometric testing of the Chinese version has established content, construct, convergent and discriminate validity w24,25x. Recent research w4x among Chinese men with CAD reported high reliability (rs0.93). Mood state was assessed using the Profile of Mood States (POMS), a 65-item adjective checklist using a five-point Likert format (0snot at all, 4sextremely). The POMS measures six emotional dimensions (tension-anxiety, depression-dejection, anger-hostility, vigor-activity, fatigue-inertia, and confusionbewilderment) and total mood disturbance w26x. The total mood disturbance score is obtained after subtracting the vigor-activity subscale from the sum of the remaining five subscales (58 items). The remaining seven items in the POMS are not calculated into the mood disturbance score. Possible scores range from y32 to 200, with higher scores indicating greater mood disturbance. The POMS is a widely used instrument with established psychometric properties. Translation and independent back-translation of the POMS into Chinese, found an 89% agreement with the original version w4x. Psychometric testing of the Chinese version has established content, convergent and divergent validity with strong test-retest reliability (rs0.86) reported w27x. Strong reliability (rs0.91) using Cronbach's alpha coefficient, among a Chinese CC population has also been reported w4x. The construct of uncertainty was measured by Mishel's Uncertainty in Illness Scale (MUIS) containing 33 items on a five-point Likert format (1sstrongly disagree, 5sstrongly agree). Possible scores range from 33 to 165, with higher scores indicating greater uncertainty. It has been widely used among diverse clinical populations to assess uncertainty. Normative data among cardiac patients have reported alpha reliability coefficients of 0.86-0.91. Construct, discriminate and convergent validity have also been reported w21x. Psychometric testing of the Chinese version has established construct, concurrent, convergent and discriminate validity, with good internal consistency estimates (rs0.74-0.86) w28,29x. Recent research among Chinese men hospitalized for cardiac catheterization has found similar results w4x. Physiologic measures included heart and respiratory rates. Heart and respiratory rates were taken manually for 1 min on all participants by the same research assistant.Procedure Subjects meeting the inclusion criteria were individually approached by the research assistant and invited to participate in this pilot study. The research assistant briefly explained the purpose of the study. Those willing to participate signed a consent form. After written consent was obtained, the subject was randomly assigned to one of the three groups in the study, by drawing a slip of paper with group assignment. All subjects were asked to complete an initial set of questionnaires and have physiological measures taken to serve as baseline data (T0). As patient anxiety is highest prior to an invasive procedure, the intervention was given 1 h before the CC procedure, lasting 15-20 min. Questionnaires were readministered and physiological measures re-taken after the intervention in order to determine whether these interventions had any effect on patient anxiety, uncertainty, mood state, heart or respiratory rate (T1). 1 h after the CC procedure (approx. 3-4 h later) questionnaires were re-administered and physiological measures re-taken in order to determine whether these interventions had any long-lasting effect (T2), as the perceived source of anxiety is the CC procedure.Experimental interventions One experienced ()10 years) registered nurse provided the interventions. Patients in the music therapy group were given a cassette recorder and headphones with individual volume control w17x. As previous research has documented the importance of preference of the listener in the selection of soothing music, subjects were given a choice of musical selections including both Chinese and Western music w6,19x. New Age, Chinese instrumental or classical music was available for selfselection. All music was instrumental without words. New Age music included 'Songs from a Secret Garden' or an 'Introduction to New World music, volume 1: Relaxing' selections. 'Guzheng selections of Kwok Wai-Sze' represented the Chinese instrumental choice, while classical music included 'Beethoven's Symphony Nr. 8' or 'Pachelbel's Canon in D'. Sensations specific to the CC procedure and preparation information have been previously developed in North America w12x. A sensation information script was translated and independently back-translated into Chinese by bilingual university educated persons. The final script was then produced for administration by audiocassette to ensure equivalent administration of the intervention to all subjects in the sensory information intervention group. Those in the sensory information group were given a cassette recorder and headphones with individual volume control prior to CC. The subjects listened to a pre-recorded audiotape providing the sensation information, describing what a patient will see, hear, smell, taste and feel during the CC w20x. Those in the control group received no added nursing intervention, however, all groups received the usual nursing care prior to CC. The usual nursing care includes monitoring vital signs, administering medications, maintaining a patent intravenous infusion, ensuring that the consent form is signed and providing brief procedural instructions about CC.Study feasibility Subjects were asked to complete a set of questionnaires at three different times. Each set of questionnaires consisted of three pages, taking approximately 15-30 min to complete. All subjects completed the questionnaires without assistance, however, some of the older subjects commented on the difficulty in reading the small print. Many subjects found difficulty in answering questions from a supine position after the CC procedure. There were no major difficulties in implementing the nursing interventions. However, the hospital environment was usually noisy, perhaps minimizing the optimal effects of the music therapy and sensory information interventions. Moreover, the hospital environment may have distracted subjects, making it difficult to concentrate when responding to the questionnaires. The attrition (17%) was due to incomplete data, which was often the result of CC scheduling, as it was subject to change, e.g. some subjects scheduled for the procedure in the afternoon actually had CC in the morning and vice versa.Data analysis Data were analyzed using the Statistical Package for the Social Sciences (SPSS 10.0) w30x. Descriptive statistics were used for all variables. Demographic data of all study subjects were compared among the three groups using Chi-square and analysis of variance (ANOVA), to ensure unbiased baseline data with regard to group assignment. Cronbach's alpha coefficients were calculated on the instruments used in this study in order to determine the internal consistency estimates of the instruments. Intention-to-treat analysis was employed, whereby all patients were analyzed in the initial groups that they were randomly assigned. In this study, no changes occurred after initial allocation. Repeated measures analysis of covariance (ANCOVA), controlling for age, was used to test for differences and compare variability between and within the three groups. Musicsmusic therapy, sensoryssensory information.Ethical issues Approval to conduct the study was obtained from the Ethics Committee of the Faculty of Medicine, University of Hong Kong, with the investigation carried out according to the principles outlined in the Declaration of Helsinki. Subjects willing to participate signed a written informed consent form. All subjects were assured of confidentially and the ability to decline participation or withdraw from the study at any time. Neither the investigators nor the research assistant gave direct patient care to subjects in the study.ResultsStudy sample A total of 54 persons agreed to participate in the study, however, nine subjects (17%) withdrew resulting in incomplete data, as they were sent to CC before completion of data collection at either time-one or timetwo. A total of 45 subjects (15ygroup) aged 37-73 (means58, S.D.s9.61) completed the study, results reported are from these subjects. Greater than two-thirds of the subjects were male (ns36, 80%), married (ns 41, 91%), with at least a secondary level of education (ns31, 69%) and no stated religious beliefs (ns31, 69%). Approximately half of the subjects (ns23, 51%) were blue-collar workers (e.g. salesperson, security guard, technician), while the others were either retired (ns13, 29%) or white-collar workers (e.g. engineer, civil servant, manager) (ns9, 20%). Subjects were diagnosed with either acute coronary syndrome (ns23, 51%), angina (ns20, 44%) or an irregular heart rhythm (ns2, 4%). Two-thirds of the subjects had a history of hypertension andyor diabetes mellitus (ns30, 67%) (Table 1 ). Among the subjects randomly assigned to the music therapy group, New Age music was the most frequently self-selected type of music (ns7, 47%), followed by Chinese instrumental (ns5, 33%) and classical (ns3, 20%). Study participant's mean scores are reported in Table 2 for the anxiety, uncertainty, mood state, heart and respiratory rates at all three times. Examination of the mood disturbance subscale scores was done at all time points, given the variability in the total mood disturbances mean scores between the groups. ANOVAs revealed a significant difference in reported fatigueinertia (F s3.87, Ps0.029, h s0.16) and confu-2 2,42 sion-bewilderment (F s3.44, Ps0.041, h s0.14) 2 2,42 following the CC procedure (T3). The post-hoc analysis using Tukey's HSD test found that the sensory information group reported less fatigue-inertia (means1.93, S.D.s2.66, Ps0.023) and confusion-bewilderment (means3.33, S.D.s1.71, Ps0.055) than the control group (means6.60, S.D.s5.50; means5.87, S.D.s 3.48, respectively). However, there were no significant differences in total mood disturbance scores reported, at each time point, between the groups. Despite randomization, age differences were found between the music therapy, sensory information and control groups. ANOVA examining age revealed a significant difference (F s8.25, Ps0.001, h s0.28) 2 2,42 between the groups. The post-hoc analysis using Tukey's HSD test found that the control group (means65, S.D.s6.88) was significantly older than both the music therapy (means57, S.D.s10.29, Ps0.028) and sensory information (means53, S.D.s7.43, Ps0.001) groups. The sensory information and music therapy groups did not differ significantly from each other, regarding age. Chi-square found no statistically significant differences between the three groups with regard to gender, marital status, highest education level obtained, religion, occupation, diagnosis or co-morbidities. ANOVAs of all baseline-dependent variable mean scores (anxiety, uncertainty, mood state, heart and respiratory rate) revealed no significant differences between the groups. However, given the significant relationship found between age and group assignment, Pearson's correlations coefficients were utilized to examine possible relationships between age and the dependent variables in this study. Results obtained, found a significant negative correlation between age and anxiety (rsy 0.31, Ps0.04 at baseline; rsy0.30, Ps0.04 postintervention; rsy0.22, Ps0.15 post-cardiac catheterization), with older age associated with lower anxiety scores. There were no significant relationships found between age and the other dependent variables measured in this study.Instrument reliability Reliability scores for the instruments in this study were found to be high at all data collection times. Internal consistency estimates for the SAI ranged from 0.88 to 0.90, while those for the POMS ranged from 0.90 to 0.92. The internal consistency estimates for the MUIS ranged from 0.80 to 0.85, demonstrating strong reliability over time.Repeated measures Repeated measures analysis of covariance (ANCO-VA), controlling for age, was used to test for differences between and within the groups. The main effects of group, time and group-by-time interaction were tested for state-anxiety, uncertainty, mood state, heart and respiratory rate. There were no statistically significant differences in the dependent variable mean scores among the music therapy, sensory information and control groups. There were no significant decreases in stateanxiety, uncertainty, heart and respiratory rate, and improvement in mood state over time; and no significant group-by-time interaction for any of these variables. The only significant finding was that anxiety scores and age were significantly different between subjects (F s 1,41 6.90, Ps0.012, h s0.14), when testing for the main 2 effect of anxiety.Discussion The aim of this pilot study was to determine the effectiveness of nursing interventions using music therapy or sensory information among Chinese populations to reduce anxiety and uncertainty, and improve negative mood. After controlling for age, there were no significant differences between the three groups. Moreover, there were no significant decreases in anxiety, uncertainty, heart and respiratory rate and no improvement in mood state, across groups. Approximately 14% of the variance in level of anxiety could be explained by age. The dependent variables measured in this study (Table 2 ) remained fairly stable across time. Results obtained in this pilot study may partly be attributed to the interventions having a smaller effect than reported in previous studies. Moreover, the small sample in this pilot study likely had insufficient power to detect differences between groups. Information regarding current medication regime of the participants was overlooked, though many study subjects had a history of hypertension. It is feasible that subjects were taking beta-adrengeric blockers (e.g. propranolol), as their mean heart rates were within a normal range limit. It is possible that unknown effects of medication, masked the physiological changes associated with a relaxation response. Further, patients are commonly given anti-anxiety medication (e.g. alprazolam or diazepam) prior to an invasive procedure, such as CC. Alprazolam and diazepam are thought to reduce anxiety by affecting the limbic system w31x, the same pathway music is thought to utilize for reducing anxiety w17x. Moreover, baseline mean anxiety scores ranged from low to moderate levels w8x, suggesting that subjects were not experiencing much anxiety. Many older subjects mentioned having had a prior cardiac catheterization, which may have affected their level of anxiety, though omitted from the data collected. Previous cardiac catheterization experience may be a possible confounding variable, as previous exposure to a stressor, such as the CC procedure, may mediate the effects of the stressor, with a resultant reduction in perceived stress and anxiety w23x. Information and communication between the cardiologist and subjects regarding findings obtained during CC was not monitored, though this also may have affected perceived anxiety. The findings in this study are in contrast to previous research among subjects with CAD, which have reported reductions in anxiety following either a music intervention w7,13x or sensory information w2,14x. Further, improvements in mood following a single music intervention, among subjects admitted for CAD was previously reported w8,19x. However, these studies are limited to persons in North America. Only the study by Elliott w6x among patients with CAD and living in western culture has reported non-significant findings, following a music intervention aimed at reducing anxiety. The results were partly explained by the low power w6x. Traditional Chinese philosophies of Taoism, Confucianism and Buddhism provide the framework for perspectives on life, health, and social interactions w32,33x. Chinese strive to be in harmony and balance within themselves, their social relationships and the universe. This means that it is vital to avoid negativism, emotional outbursts, practice of self-restraint and prudence w33x. Moreover, there is a cultural stigma associated with emotional distress, with a tendency among Chinese to emphasize somatic aspects of illness w34x. Emotions are thought to affect all parts of the body, due to the interconnection between emotions and internal organs through meridians and the balance of chi, or vital energy w35x. Excessive emotion, such as anxiety, fear, or worry, are thought to cause disharmony, thus allowing disease to occur w36x. A balance of emotions is important to maintain proper functioning of the vital organs. This suggests that for Chinese persons, emotions or strong personal feelings must be contained and controlled in social situations, even those that are perceived as stressful w32x. The cultural expectation of self-control, or suppression of strong feelings is consistent with the value of balance that is integral to Chinese worldviews and social systems w37x. According to Confucian doctrine it is important to conduct oneself according to the established social and cultural norms for public behavior and emotional responses, as to not cause shame on oneself or family w4,37x. Personal actions reflect not only on the individual, but on the whole extended family. Maintaining 'face' is vital to Chinese, as it ensures the entire family is not judged negatively by society. Loss of face, through an emotional outburst or public loss of self-control results in shame w33,37x. Chinese may not honestly express their true discomfort or feelings, for fear of causing a social disgrace and 'losing face' w32x. Further research may help determine whether differences in cultural background and beliefs, affect reported responses or perceptions toward the effectiveness these nursing interventions.Strengths and limitations Independent nursing interventions for Chinese persons with CAD was found to be feasible in this study. Information gained in this study regarding study design improvement, can be incorporated into future research and may help alleviate unexplained observations or confounding associated with older age, medications or previous CC experience. Subjects in this study used self-report questionnaires for determining perceived anxiety, uncertainty and mood state. Possible response bias may have been introduced into the results, with subjects answering extreme choices on the rank-order scales. Further, subjects may have given socially desired responses, in order not to 'lose face'. As Chinese tend to somatize emotional distress, including anxiety and depression, perhaps a measurement of somatization could be developed and used in future studies. Randomization is intended to equally distribute possible confounding variables, to ensure comparable groups at baseline. Despite randomization, age differences were found between the three groups. The control group was significantly older than the two experimental groups, with older subjects reporting less anxiety, likely affecting the study results. Information bias may have been introduced into the study, as randomization occurred before the collection of baseline data. Finally, the sample size for this study is likely to have been inadequate for detecting any differences and may have been a factor for the non-significant findings, affecting the power of the study, the ability to detect a difference if one exists and allowed for a possible Type 2 error.Recommendations for future research In future studies, randomization stratified by age may be advisable, as age and anxiety correlated significantly in this sample. Further, randomization of subjects should be done after baseline data is collected and independent from the researcher conducting the intervention, in order to obtain 'blinded' baseline data unbiased by group assignment. Questionnaires with larger print or allowing subjects to answer questionnaires verbally may reduce practical concerns voiced by some subjects. This is especially important for older subjects and those required to remain in a supine position for long periods, following CC. Limiting this type of research study to patients undergoing their first cardiac catheterization may help avoid the potential confounding effects associated with prior exposure. Future research could be done comparing responses to anxiety-reducing nursing interventions among Chinese and Western persons with CAD simultaneously, to see whether these interventions are equally effective or whether there are differences in the ways Chinese perceive stressful situations and negative emotions. Further, providing subjects with the opportunity to verbalize their views about whether music therapy or sensory information was beneficial, or if they would recommend this type of intervention to others, would provide additional information and insight for utilizing these interventions.Conclusion Unrelieved anxiety can produce an increase in sympathetic nervous system activity leading to an increase in cardiac workload. title: Specially Selected Music in the Cardiac Laboratory—an Important Tool for Improvement of the Wellbeing of Patients
abstract: Purpose: To investigate the effect of a specially selected music sound environment on the feeling of wellbeing of adult, lightly sedated patients in a Cardiac Catheter Laboratory undergoing invasive procedures. Method: Patients (ns193) were randomly assigned to either a music group, who listened to music during the procedure (ns99) or to a non-music group (ns94). Immediately after the procedure all patients were interviewed by a questionnaire about their opinion of the sound environment in the room and about their feeling of wellbeing. Results: In the music group 91% of the patients defined the sound environment as very pleasantypleasant -compared to 56% in the non-music group. The number of patients with 'no opinion' on the sound environment was lower in the music group than in the non-music group (8% vs. 42%). In the non-music group only 34% of the patients would have liked to listen to music, if possible, whereas 82% of the patients in the music group were very pleasedy pleased with the music. Both groups noticed basic sounds and noises with similar frequencies. In the music group 62% of the patients noticed the music spontaneously. Sixty-eight patients (68%) reported that music was of major positive importance to their feeling of wellbeing. These patients expressed that music made them feel less tense, more relaxed and safe. The results were not related to age, sex or procedure. Conclusion: Specially selected music had a positive effect on the wellbeing of patients and their opinion on the sound environment during invasive cardiac procedures. Based on the negative expectations and the positive experience of the patients with regard to music environment, we suggest that specially selected music should be a part of the sound environment in the Cardiac Catheter Laboratory.

main_text: Introduction Music has since ancient time been a well known and acknowledged tool used for healing sickness, reducing anxiety and stress and increasing the feeling of wellbeing w1x. Recently, a review w11x of the literature has found that 'music reduces anxiety and respiratory rate in hospital patients'. However, despite the evidence for the use of music as a valuable tool to increase the physical and psychological wellbeing of patients, its use in hospital settings is surprisingly uncommon and unstructured. The effect of music used as part of therapy in relation to patients undergoing coronary by-pass surgery has been described by others w3,4x. In addition, several studies of patients suffering from acute myocardial infarction w5-8x have documented the relaxing effect of music with reduction of heart rate, respiratory rate and state of anxiety. No previous studies have described the effect of music in relation to the feeling of wellbeing of patients during coronary procedures at a Cardiac Catheter Laboratory. As Percutaneous Coronary Intervention (PCI) is a rapidly increasing option of treatment in relation to coronary vessel disease, it is as such an interesting area to study. During the procedure the patients are just lightly sedated and therefore very much at risk for anxiety and stress. Our hypothesizes were: • Patients will benefit from listening to specially selected music. • Music will be able to increase the patients feeling of wellbeing. • Music will distract their focus on unpleasant sounds and noises in the X-ray room.MethodsSetting Our Cardiac Laboratory is equipped with two almost identical X-ray rooms for coronary procedures. The Xray rooms are equipped with technical equipmentsuch as movable X-ray equipment, ceiling suspended monitors, computers, defibrillators and other resuscitation equipment. To most patients this equipment is unfamiliar and unknown, and perhaps increases the feeling of anxiety and stress. During the study X-ray room 1 (the non-music room) represented our usual 'basic sounds', which were the sounds and noises from the staff (three nurses and one cardiologist), the heartbeat of the patient through loudspeakers (to be heard by everyone in the room and to be used clinically by the staff), unpacking of sterile equipment, telephones, talking, ventilation, the noise from the movable X-ray equipment, etc. During the study period we made no changes in our 'basic sounds'. X-Ray room 2 represented the music sound environment, which consisted of 'basic sounds' with superimposed selected music played via ceiling suspended loudspeakers. Thus, we changed the sound environment in the music room by adding music to the 'basic sounds'. This study examined the effects of music played via ceiling suspended loudspeakers during coronary procedures. We chose ceiling suspended loudspeakers (in stead of headphones or other sound sources), as dialogue with the patient during the procedure is part of our reference procedure. In addition our choice of music and our philosophy concerning the non-dominance of designed sound-and music environment demands a distant sound source. Our investigation focused primarily on the individual, subjective judgement and experience of the persons tested-with special focus on a possible subjective correlation between music and wellbeing. The patients were consecutively included into the study and randomly assigned to the music and the nonmusic group. The patients were not asked or informed in advance about the study of the sound environment or about playing music, and we did not draw the attention of the patients to the fact that there was music in the X-ray room. In order to avoid any bias the sound environment was switched between the two rooms after inclusion of the first 100 patients. The protocol for the study was approved by the local ethical committee of the County of North Jutland, Denmark.The music The music genre was chosen for the study from the experience of previous studies w5,9,10x, after having listened to numerable CD's and gained advice from the composer Niels Eje, Denmark, who creates music specially designed for patients in hospital settings. This music is meant to be soft, image-creating and stimulating, and it is meant to provide a relaxing atmosphere. The composition of his music is designed to bypass the taste of ages, sexes and general opinion of genres of music and consists of a symbiosis of sounds from nature and specially composed music. We played soft classical music (Trio Rococo: 'Friends', Trio Rococo: 'Beatles Classical Mystery Tour', Niels Eje: 'Day and Night', Kare Norge: 'Morning has broken', Mikael Beier: 'Tagen letter', Romantic Bach, Richard Clayderman: 'Scandinavian collection' and Bolivian panflute-music) and music composed by the composer Niels Eje. We made a specific order of the Compact Discs (CD) to be played, and the volume was fixed at a specified level, which made the music just audible. In other studies relaxing music has been defined as 'primarily of string composition, low-pitched, having a simple and direct musical rhythm, and haing a tempo of approximately 60 beats per minute' w6x. The music was played via ceiling suspended loudspeakers, as this sound source enables verbal communication with the patient during the procedure. Active communication with the patient is part of our standard procedure, and headphones or sound pillows might compromise this option. Furthermore our choice of soft classical, instrumental and non-dominant music requires-in our opinion-a non-dominant sound source. Other studies w3,4,10x used headphones, and their argument was that they wanted 'to block out environmental noises' w4x. The patients and the staff involved had no influence on the choice of music, which was selected by the investigators of the study. There are several recommendations of the best suitable genre of music for patients in hospital settings w10,13x, but there are no evidence-based recommendations in this field.Design The patients were hospitalized on the day of the cardiac procedure. They were pre-medicated by an oral dose of 5 mg diazepam and were transferred to the Cardiac Catheter Laboratory 1 h later. All patients undergoing a coronary procedure during the period of the study were consecutively included. Patients were excluded, if they were hard of hearing, did not understand the Danish language, asked for the  music to be turned off (in the music group) or asked for music to be played (in the non-music group). The patients were interviewed by a questionnaire immediately after the cardiac procedure at the Cardiac Catheter Laboratory by nurses, who were specifically trained in how and when to question the patients. Specific guidelines were made to ensure that all patients were asked the same questions-and to ensure that the patients' statements were standardized in the questionnaire. The questionnaire focused on the following items: • The patients opinion of the sound environment (very pleasantypleasantyunpleasantyvery unpleasantyno opinion). • The sounds spontaneously noticed in the room during the procedure (telephone, unpacking, music, heartbeat, talking etc). • The experience of the music in the room (very pleasantypleasantyunpleasantyvery unpleasantyno opinion). • The effect of the music in relation to their feeling of wellbeing (positive effectysome effectyindifferent effectynegative effectyno opinion). • The like or dislike of the music they heard (the music group)-or 'would they have liked to listen to music-if possible' (the non-music group). The patients were asked to motivate their answer. We did not define the phenomenon 'feeling of wellbeing' to the patients. The feeling of wellbeing is a very subjective, relative and easily changeable feeling. In our study we allowed this possible wide range of subjective responses and interpretations of how to express the feeling of wellbeing -although we were fully aware of the limitations of the results of such a wide range of possibly various interpretations.ResultsDemographics There were included 193 patients, men (64%) and women (36%), and the average of age for both sexes was 62 years-ranging from 30-89 years. There were 99 patients in music group and 94 patients in the nonmusic group. For statistical treatment of data we used Dunn's multiple comparison test. In the music group 68 patients (69%) experienced that music was of major positive importance for their feeling of wellbeing. Seven patients (7%) expressed that the music was of minor positive importance. None experienced a negative correlation between music and wellbeing, whereas 24 patients (24%) had no opinion of the correlation of music and the feeling of wellbeing. In the music group 90 patients (91%) found that the sound environment was pleasant compared to 53 patients (56%) in the non-music group. One patient (1%) found that the sound environment was unpleasant in music group, and no patients (0%) found the sound environment unpleasant in non-music group (N.S.). Eight patients (8%) in the music group had no opinion on the sound environment compared to 41 (44%) with no opinion in the non-music group (P-0.05) (Fig. 1 ). There was no significant difference between the observations of the sounds in the two sound environments (Table 1 ). Sixty-two percent of the patients in the music group noticed the music spontaneously-although they were not notified about the presence of music in the room in advance. In the non-music group all patients were asked if they would have liked to listen to music during the procedure, if it had been an option to them. Only 34% of these patients would accept this offer. In the music group, however, 88% of the patients found that it was very pleasantypleasant to listen to the music during the procedure (Fig. 2 ). The patients were asked to motivate their opinion of the effect of the music played. The patients said: 'The music made me feel relaxed', 'I felt safe', 'It gave me a good feeling' and 'It helped me think of other things'.Discussion At the Cardiac Catheter Laboratory we focus on patient environment. We care about the surroundings of the patient with regard to sounds, colors, light intensity and temperature. Thus the sound environment is for us a natural and obvious parameter to watch. When arriving to the Cardiac Laboratory our lightly sedated, but awake patients meet a world of total strangeness: X-ray equipment, computers, and sterile rooms. Laboratory staffs are dressed up in sterile clothing with caps and masks. The laboratory represents an area of unfamiliar sounds and noises from the X-ray equipment, unpacking of sterile equipment, contrast-syringe, instruments and sounds from the use of computers -as well as a massive hi-tech visual impression. It is of major importance to focus on improvement of the quality of care -also with regard to the surroundings. As noted by Heitz w10x: 'It is nice to experience something familiar, music, when so much of what a person experiences in the hospital is not'.The music In our study 82% of the patients found that the music chosen was very pleasantypleasant-although they had no influence on the choice of music. The fact that the majority of our patients reported that they liked the music they heard may be explained by: the source of the sound, the type of music played and the level of the sound. Music played via ceiling suspended loudspeakers (and not headphones or sound pillows) does not isolate the patient from staff and surroundings. The music will be at a pleasant 'distance' and will never be disturbing or 'dangerous' (e.g. emotionally provocative). This applies to the level of the sound as well. We choose a sound level just audible, which made the music a supplement to the other sounds in the room. The music never claimed the attention of the patient. Finally, the music was specially selected. The composer Niels Eje, Denmark creates music specially designed for patients in hospital settings, and he is part of the ensemble Trio Rococo. His music is meant to be soft, image-creating and stimulating, and it is meant to provide a relaxing atmosphere. The composition of his music is designed to bypass the taste of ages, sexes and general opinion of genres of music and consists of a symbiosis of sounds from nature and special composed music. In this study we played several pieces of his compositions together with selected classical pieces, which fulfilled the same criteria. Although no evidence-based recommendations are available for the choice of the music, it is clear that this is an important factor. The right music for the purpose in question should be selected either by following the patient's own wish or by following the advice of experienced composers of designed music-and sound environment for hospital patients. In this study the music genre was selected so as to bypass all music preferences related to age, sex and taste. Music was selected by applying and adapting the theories of the music organization 'Musica Humana' w14x-a project-organization that organizes and initiates studies of sound environment in hospitals. This organization is committed to creating and documenting a new sound environment for patients. The studies of the organization analyze the use of a created sound-and music environment as a supplement to the basic sounds. Others have recommended that the patients choose the genre of music individually, e.g. O'Sullivan w13x, Heitz w10x and Bolwerk et al. w5xbased on the opinion that 'The nature of the music will determine the effect the music has on the listener' w5x, 'Individuals will react differently to the same music...' w10x and 'Music should be adapted to each patient's needs«' w13x. This study has showed, however, that it is possible to select music that will satisfy and be useful for the majority of the patients-at least within the category of the cardiac patient group. However, in order to learn more about this field further studies about the effects of various sound sources, types of music and the choice of the music level are required.Patients observation of the sounds The results of this study showed no difference between our two patient groups with respect to the frequency of spontaneously notified sounds (Table 1 ). We expected that the observation of the basic sounds in the room would be less in the music sound environment compared to the basic sound environment. Our expectations were a.o. based on O'Sullivan w13x who states: 'If ones attention focuses on the music all other stimuli will be cancelled, therefore providing distraction therapy Downloaded from https://academic.oup.com/eurjcn/article/3/1/21/5931631 by TU Wien Bibliothek user on 30 May 2023 reducing awareness of the sounds of ventilators and alarms', and Heitz w10x: 'there was a clinical significance with a tendency for music to filter away unpleasant sounds'. Although 62% of the patients in the music group noticed the music, the awareness of other sounds remained unchanged (Table 1 ). Nevertheless, our study showed a clear difference between the patients' opinion of the sound environment in the two rooms-with the only real difference being the addition of music to the basic sounds in the room (Fig. 1 ). These results support the hypothesis, that certain types of music may be superimposed on the basic sound environment in a nondominant way and still have a major impact on patients. The choice of the sound source (loudspeakers) enabled the patients to communicate, follow the procedure, but gave them no chance to shut out the sounds and noises in the room. Still the experience of the sound environment was much more positive in the music group compared to the non-music group -although the basic sounds were the same (Fig. 1 ). Fig. 1 shows the great difference between the number of patients with no opinion of the sound environment in the music (8%) and the non-music room (44%). In the music room remarkably few patients have no opinion compared to the music room. Perhaps music sharpens their senses or makes them aware of the sounds in the room. This difference needs further investigation.Patients expectations vs. patients experience Most patients had negative expectations with regard to listening to music in relation to their stay at the Cardiac Laboratory (Fig. 2 ). They had no relevant insight into the positive effects that music could bring to them in relation to the cardiac procedure. We conclude that we need not ask the patients for the permission of playing specially selected music-as their expectations do not match their experience (Fig. 2 ). Given that the music selected is appropriate, the majority of the patients will have a positive experience-regardless of their expectations. It may indeed be difficult for the patients to choose the type of music best suited for the coronary procedure-as they have no impression of their needs in the given situation. Many of our patients agreed that the music they heard during the coronary intervention was perfect for this use, but they might be reluctant to listen to this genre of music at home. These results suggest that specially selected or composed music should be chosen for a given therapeutic situation, and that patient permission for playing the music need not be sought. Several studies have examined the effects of music with focus on physiological and psychological changes w1, 3,4,6,10,11,13x , and there seems to be a great variety of the measured effects. In other studies w3,4,10x the design of the study was based upon self selected music or limited self selected music (the patients were given the option of a limited number of investigator selected music pieces) played via headphones. In our study we wanted to get closer to which kind of music is the best suitable for a given therapeutic situation, and we therefore chose investigator selected music. This choice might of course have resulted in negative experiences from some of the patients, but as 82% of the patients liked the music chosen (Fig. 2 ), we find that this result is satisfactory. In review of literature we found other studies which were based upon investigator selected music w5,6,12x. Their choice of music was likewise based upon the positive experience of previous studies.Focus on psychological benefits Several studies in current literature have examined the psychological effect of music by using Spielberger's state-trait anxiety instrument (STAI) w2x as a tool to evaluate the patients' responses. In our study we did not focus on anxiety, but instead on the patients' statements on the feeling of wellbeing and experience of the sound environment, and it is therefore difficult to make any comparisons with other studies. Most cardiac patients are medicated with drugs which reduce the pulse and blood pressure, and the measurement of these parameters might be of uncertain value in relation to the effect of music. This study has therefore focused on the psychological benefits of listening to music. In addition, we find it of great importance as a nursing intervention to focus on the possible improvement of the psychological feeling of wellbeing of our patients. We have learned from this study that specially selected or composed music may be a strong and positive factor for patients' wellbeing and experience of the sound environment. The results of this study support the fact that music is a useful tool for improved patient care. When applying music in a hospital environment White w6x states: 'it takes very little time to implement, it is inexpensive, and it is an intervention the clinical nurse specialist can help patients to use independently in a very short time.' In a Cardiac Laboratory patients suffer many unavoidable stressors. Application of therapeutic intervention such as specially selected music can help minimizing these stressors, improving patient comfort and possibly morbidity. Additionally, further studies of the waiting period before and after the cardiac procedure ought to be performed. This period of time is likewise for many patients filled with anxiety and discomfort. Through the review of literature we found that this field has been briefly investigated w1,5,10,15x, but it needs more focus.Conclusion This study has shown that it is possible to improve the sound environment by the use of specially selected or composed music superimposed on the basic sound environment. The use of music improved the patients' experience of their stay in our laboratory. Based on these results medical staff in the Cardiac Catheter Laboratory environment ought to consider the sound environment. We suggest that the use of specially selected music should be considered in order to improve the sound environment. Finally, we suggest that further studies should be made in order to develop protocols for the use of music before, during and after cardiac procedures in the Cardiac Catheter Laboratory and in order to test various genres of music and sound sources best suitable for the purpose.Fig.1. The patients' experience of the sound environment expressed as 'very pleasant'y'pleasant'-'unpleasantyvery unpleasant'-'no opinion' when exposed to basic sound or music sound. There are significantly less patients with 'no opinion' in the music group compared to the non-music group.Fig. 2 . 2 Fig. 2. In basic sound environment (left columns) most patients declined (said 'No' to the question: 'Would you have liked to listen to music, if possible?') the hypothetical offer of listening to music during the cardiac procedure, whereas the vast majority of the patients exposed to music sound environment (right columns) expressed that they liked (said 'Yes' -very muchyfairlyynot muchynot at allydo not know-to the question: 'Did you like the music in the room during the procedure').Table 1 1 Which sounds did the patients notice? title: Music Interventions in Patients During Coronary Angiographic Procedures: A Randomized Controlled Study of the Effect on Patients' Anxiety and Well-Being
abstract: Background: A cardiac catheterization laboratory can be a frightening environment and music can be a supportive source of environmental sound that stimulates and maintains relaxation. Aim: To test the effects of patient focused music versus loudspeaker music versus standard sound on patient's experiences of anxiety and well-being during coronary angiographic procedures. Methods: A prospective, randomized, controlled trial of 98 subjects undergoing elective coronary angiogram and/or percutaneous coronary intervention. The subjects were randomly allocated to three different groups of sound environments: a control group (the usual sound environment), a patient focused music group (audio pillow) or to a loudspeaker music group. Results: Anxiety decreased significantly and well-being increased significantly in the two music groups compared to the control group. There was a significantly more positive impression of the sound environment in the patient focused music group compared to the two other groups. Conclusion: This study showed that the use of a specially designed music reduced anxiety and increased well-being in patients during coronary angiographic procedures. However, patient focused music seemed to be more preferable. The sound environment was rated more positively by the subjects listening to music via audio pillow. The music delivered via loudspeakers seemed to distract the staff during the examination at the cardiac catheterization laboratory.
